title: Profound neuronal differences during Exercise-Induced Hypoalgesia between athletes and non-athletes revealed by functional near-infrared spectroscopy
description: This functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) dataset investigates neuronal mechanisms underlying exercise-induced hypoalgesia in endurance athletes versus non-athletes. Twenty-seven endurance athletes and twenty-five non-athletes participated in the study. Participants underwent high-intensity interval training (HIIT) and pressure pain testing while brain oxygenation was monitored in pain-processing regions including the prefrontal cortex, sensorimotor cortices, and posterior parietal cortex. The study reveals distinct neural activation patterns and pain reduction responses between groups, suggesting that endurance training adaptations enhance neural efficiency during high-intensity exercise.
methods_description: Twenty-seven endurance athletes (mean age: 33.3 ± 10.8 years) and twenty-five non-athletes (mean age: 28.9 ± 9.0 years) participated in the study. Participants underwent high-intensity interval training (HIIT) and pressure pain tests while functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) monitored brain oxygenation in key pain-processing regions: prefrontal cortex (PFC), sensorimotor cortices, and posterior parietal cortex (PPC). Oxyhemoglobin levels were measured during exercise and post-exercise painful stimulation. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants before the study. This study conformed to the standards set by the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Faculty of Social and Behavioral Sciences, Friedrich Schiller University Jena (FSV 23/006).
